# Quart

```{image} https://raw.githubusercontent.com/pallets/quart/main/artwork/logo.png
:alt: Quart logo
```

{{ Build Status }} {{ docs }} {{ pypi }} {{ python }} {{ license }} {{ chat }}

Quart is an async Python web microframework. Using Quart you can,

- render and serve HTML templates,
- write (RESTful) JSON APIs,
- serve WebSockets,
- stream request and response data,
- do pretty much anything over the HTTP or WebSocket protocols.

## Quickstart

Quart can be installed via [pip](https://docs.python.org/3/installing/index.html),

```console
$ pip install quart
```

and requires Python 3.8.0 or higher (see [python version support](https://quart.palletsprojects.com/en/latest/discussion/python_versions.html)
for reasoning).

A minimal Quart example is,

```python
from quart import Quart, render_template, websocket

app = Quart(__name__)

@app.route("/")
async def hello():
    return await render_template("index.html")

@app.route("/api")
async def json():
    return {"hello": "world"}

@app.websocket("/ws")
async def ws():
    while True:
        await websocket.send("hello")
        await websocket.send_json({"hello": "world"})

if __name__ == "__main__":
    app.run()
```

if the above is in a file called `app.py` it can be run as,

```console
$ python app.py
```

To deploy this app in a production setting see the [deployment](https://quart.palletsprojects.com/en/latest/tutorials/deployment.html)
documentation.

## Contributing

Quart is developed on [GitHub](https://github.com/pallets/quart). If
you come across an issue, or have a feature request please open an
[issue](https://github.com/pallets/quart/issues). If you want to
contribute a fix or the feature-implementation please do (typo fixes
welcome), by proposing a [merge request](https://github.com/pallets/quart/pulls).

### Testing

The best way to test Quart is with [Tox](https://tox.readthedocs.io),

```console
$ pip install tox
$ tox
```

this will check the code style and run the tests.

## Help

The Quart [documentation](https://quart.palletsprojects.com) or
[cheatsheet](https://quart.palletsprojects.com/en/latest/reference/cheatsheet.html)
are the best places to start, after that try searching [stack overflow](https://stackoverflow.com/questions/tagged/quart) or ask for help
[on discord](https://discord.gg/pallets). If you still
can't find an answer please [open an issue](https://github.com/pallets/quart/issues).

## Relationship with Flask

Quart is an asyncio reimplementation of the popular [Flask](https://flask.palletsprojects.com) microframework API. This means that if you
understand Flask you understand Quart.

Like Flask, Quart has an ecosystem of extensions for more specific
needs. In addition a number of the Flask extensions work with Quart.

### Migrating from Flask

It should be possible to migrate to Quart from Flask by a find and
replace of `flask` to `quart` and then adding `async` and
`await` keywords. See the [docs](https://quart.palletsprojects.com/en/latest/how_to_guides/flask_migration.html)
for more help.

variables.name: quart.typing.AppOrBlueprintKey
documentation: The App key is None, whereas blueprints are named

functions.name: quart.helpers.get_debug_flag
documentation:
Reads QUART_DEBUG environment variable to determine whether to run
the app in debug mode. If unset, and development mode has been
configured, it will be enabled automatically.

functions.name: quart.helpers.get_load_dotenv
documentation:
Get whether the user has disabled loading default dotenv files by
setting {envvar}`QUART_SKIP_DOTENV`. The default is `True`, load
the files.
\:param default: What to return if the env var isn't set.

functions.name: quart.helpers.make_response
documentation:
Create a response, a simple wrapper function.

This is most useful when you want to alter a Response before
returning it, for example

```python
response = make_response(render_template('index.html'))
response.headers['X-Header'] = 'Something'
```

functions.name: quart.helpers.make_push_promise
documentation:
Create a push promise, a simple wrapper function.

This takes a path that should be pushed to the client if the
protocol is HTTP/2.

functions.name: quart.helpers.flash
documentation:
Add a message (with optional category) to the session store.

This is typically used to flash a message to a user that will be
stored in the session and shown during some other request. For
example,

```python
@app.route('/login', methods=['POST'])
async def login():
    ...
    await flash('Login successful')
    return redirect(url_for('index'))
```

allows the index route to show the flashed messages, without
having to accept the message as an argument or otherwise.  See
{func}`~quart.helpers.get_flashed_messages` for message retrieval.

functions.name: quart.helpers.get_flashed_messages
documentation:
Retrieve the flashed messages stored in the session. This is mostly useful in templates where it is exposed as a global function, for example ```html+jinja
    {% for message in get_flashed_messages() %}
  • {{ message }}
    • {% endfor %}
``` Note that caution is required for usage of `category_filter` as all messages will be popped, but only those matching the filter returned. See {func}`~quart.helpers.flash` for message creation.

functions.name: quart.helpers.get_template_attribute
documentation:
Load a attribute from a template.

This is useful in Python code in order to use attributes in
templates.

Arguments:
    template_name: To load the attribute from.
    attribute: The attribute name to load

functions.name: quart.helpers.url_for
documentation:
Return the url for a specific endpoint.

This is most useful in templates and redirects to create a URL
that can be used in the browser.

Arguments:

: endpoint: The endpoint to build a url for, if prefixed with

  : `.` it targets endpoint's in the current blueprint.

  \_anchor: Additional anchor text to append (i.e. #text).
  \_external: Return an absolute url for external (to app) usage.
  \_method: The method to consider alongside the endpoint.
  \_scheme: A specific scheme to use.
  values: The values to build into the URL, as specified in

  > the endpoint rule.

functions.name: quart.helpers.stream_with_context
quart.helpers.find_package
documentation: Finds packages install prefix (or None) and it's containing Folder

functions.name: quart.helpers.send_from_directory
documentation:
Send a file from a given directory.

Arguments:
: directory: Directory that when combined with file_name gives

  : the file path.

  file_name: File name that when combined with directory gives

  : the file path.

See {func}`send_file` for the other arguments.

functions.name: quart.helpers.send_file
documentation:
Return a Response to send the filename given.

Arguments:

: filename_or_io: The filename (path) to send, remember to use

  : {func}`safe_join`.

  mimetype: Mimetype to use, by default it will be guessed or

  : revert to the DEFAULT_MIMETYPE.

  as_attachment: If true use the attachment filename in a

  : Content-Disposition attachment header.

  attachment_filename: Name for the filename, if it differs
  add_etags: Set etags based on the filename, size and

  > modification time.

  last_modified: Used to override the last modified value.
  cache_timeout: Time in seconds for the response to be cached.

functions.name: quart.helpers.abort
documentation: Raise an HTTPException for the given status code.

functions.name: quart.helpers.redirect
documentation: Redirect to the location with the status code.

functions.name: quart.utils.run_sync
documentation:
Ensure that the sync function is run within the event loop.

If the *func* is not a coroutine it will be wrapped such that
it runs in the default executor (use loop.set_default_executor
to change). This ensures that synchronous functions do not
block the event loop.

functions.name: quart.templating.render_template
documentation:
Render the template with the context given.

Arguments:
    template_name_or_list: Template name to render of a list of
        possible template names.
    context: The variables to pass to the template.

functions.name: quart.templating.render_template_string
documentation:
Render the template source with the context given.

Arguments:
    source: The template source code.
    context: The variables to pass to the template.

functions.name: quart.templating.stream_template
documentation:
Render a template by name with the given context as a stream.

This returns an iterator of strings, which can be used as a
streaming response from a view.

Arguments:
    template_name_or_list: The name of the template to render. If a
        list is given, the first name to exist will be rendered.
    context: The variables to make available in the template.

functions.name: quart.templating.stream_template_string
documentation:
Render a template from the given source with the *context* as a stream.

This returns an iterator of strings, which can
be used as a streaming response from a view.

Arguments:
    source: The source code of the template to render.
    context: The variables to make available in the template.

classes.name: quart.templating.Environment

methods.name: quart.templating.Environment.__init__
documentation:
Create a Quart specific Jinja Environment.

Arguments:
    app: The Quart app to bind to.
    options: The standard Jinja Environment options.

Quart specific Jinja Environment.

This changes the default Jinja loader to use the
DispatchingJinjaLoader, and enables async Jinja by default.

functions.name: quart.testing.utils.make_test_headers_path_and_query_string
documentation:
Make the headers and path with defaults for testing.

Arguments:
    app: The application to test against.
    path: The path to request. If the query_string argument is not
        defined this argument will be partitioned on a '?' with
        the following part being considered the query_string.
    headers: Initial headers to send.
    query_string: To send as a dictionary, alternatively the
        query_string can be determined from the path.

functions.name: quart.testing.utils.make_test_body_with_headers
documentation:
Make the body bytes with associated headers.

Arguments:
    data: Raw data to send in the request body.
    form: Key value paired data to send form encoded in the
        request body.
    files: Key FileStorage paired data to send as file
        encoded in the request body.
    json: Data to send json encoded in the request body.

functions.name: quart.testing.utils.no_op_push
documentation:
A push promise sender that does nothing.

This is best used when creating Request instances for testing
outside of the QuartClient. The Request instance must know what to
do with push promises, and this gives it the option of doing
nothing.

classes.name: quart.sessions.SessionInterface

methods.name: quart.sessions.SessionInterface.make_null_session
documentation:
Create a Null session object.

This is used in replacement of an actual session if sessions
are not configured or active.

methods.name: quart.sessions.SessionInterface.is_null_session
documentation: Returns True is the instance is a null session.

methods.name: quart.sessions.SessionInterface.get_cookie_name
documentation: Helper method to return the Cookie Name for the App.

methods.name: quart.sessions.SessionInterface.get_cookie_domain
documentation: Helper method to return the Cookie Domain for the App.

methods.name: quart.sessions.SessionInterface.get_cookie_path
documentation: Helper method to return the Cookie path for the App.

methods.name: quart.sessions.SessionInterface.get_cookie_httponly
documentation: Helper method to return if the Cookie should be HTTPOnly for the App.

methods.name: quart.sessions.SessionInterface.get_cookie_secure
documentation: Helper method to return if the Cookie should be Secure for the App.

methods.name: quart.sessions.SessionInterface.get_cookie_samesite
documentation: Helper method to return the Cookie Samesite configuration for the App.

methods.name: quart.sessions.SessionInterface.get_expiration_time
documentation:
Helper method to return the Session expiration time.

If the session is not 'permanent' it will expire as and when
the browser stops accessing the app.

methods.name: quart.sessions.SessionInterface.should_set_cookie
documentation:
Helper method to return if the Set Cookie header should be present.

This triggers if the session is marked as modified or the app
is configured to always refresh the cookie.

methods.name: quart.sessions.SessionInterface.open_session
documentation:
Open an existing session from the request or create one.

Returns:

: The Session object or None if no session can be created,
  in which case the {attr}`null_session_class` is expected
  to be used.

methods.name: quart.sessions.SessionInterface.save_session
documentation:
Save the session argument to the response.

Arguments:
    response: Can be None if the session is being saved after
        a websocket connection closes.

Returns:
    The modified response, with the session stored.

Base class for session interfaces.

Attributes:
    null_session_class: Storage class for null (no storage)
        sessions.
    pickle_based: Indicates if pickling is used for the session.

classes.name: quart.sessions.SecureCookieSessionInterface

methods.name: quart.sessions.SecureCookieSessionInterface.get_signing_serializer
documentation:
Return a serializer for the session that also signs data.

This will return None if the app is not configured for secrets.

methods.name: quart.sessions.SecureCookieSessionInterface.open_session
documentation:
Open a secure cookie based session.

This will return None if a signing serializer is not available,
usually if the config SECRET_KEY is not set.

methods.name: quart.sessions.SecureCookieSessionInterface.save_session
documentation: Saves the session to the response in a secure cookie.

A Session interface that uses cookies as storage.

This will store the data on the cookie in plain text, but with a
signature to prevent modification.

variables.name: quart.app.AppOrBlueprintKey
documentation: The App key is None, whereas blueprints are named

classes.name: quart.app.Quart

methods.name: quart.app.Quart.__init__
documentation:
Construct a Quart web application.

Use to create a new web application to which requests should
be handled, as specified by the various attached url
rules. See also {class}`~quart.static.PackageStatic` for
additional constructor arguments.

Arguments:
: import_name: The name at import of the application, use

  : `__name__` unless there is a specific issue.

  host_matching: Optionally choose to match the host to the

  : configured host on request (404 if no match).

  instance_path: Optional path to an instance folder, for

  : deployment specific settings and files.

  instance_relative_config: If True load the config from a

  : path relative to the instance path.

Attributes:
: after_request_funcs: The functions to execute after a

  : request has been handled.

  after_websocket_funcs: The functions to execute after a

  : websocket has been handled.

  before_request_funcs: The functions to execute before handling

  : a request.

  before_websocket_funcs: The functions to execute before handling

  : a websocket.

methods.name: quart.app.Quart.get_send_file_max_age
documentation:
Used by {func}`send_file` to determine the `max_age` cache
value for a given file path if it wasn't passed.

By default, this returns {data}`SEND_FILE_MAX_AGE_DEFAULT` from
the configuration of {data}`~flask.current_app`. This defaults
to `None`, which tells the browser to use conditional requests
instead of a timed cache, which is usually preferable.

Note this is a duplicate of the same method in the Quart
class.

methods.name: quart.app.Quart.open_resource
documentation:
Open a file for reading.

Use as

```python
async with await app.open_resource(path) as file_:
    await file_.read()
```

methods.name: quart.app.Quart.open_instance_resource
documentation:
Open a file for reading.

Use as

```python
async with await app.open_instance_resource(path) as file_:
    await file_.read()
```

methods.name: quart.app.Quart.create_jinja_environment
documentation:
Create and return the jinja environment.

This will create the environment based on the
{attr}`jinja_options` and configuration settings. The
environment will include the Quart globals by default.

methods.name: quart.app.Quart.update_template_context
documentation:
Update the provided template context.

This adds additional context from the various template context
processors.

Arguments:
    context: The context to update (mutate).

methods.name: quart.app.Quart.before_serving
documentation:
Add a before serving function.

This will allow the function provided to be called once before
anything is served (before any byte is received).

This is designed to be used as a decorator, if used to
decorate a synchronous function, the function will be wrapped
in {func}`~quart.utils.run_sync` and run in a thread executor
(with the wrapped function returned). An example usage,

```python
@app.before_serving
async def func():
    ...
```

Arguments:

: func: The function itself.

methods.name: quart.app.Quart.while_serving
documentation:
Add a while serving generator function.

This will allow the generator provided to be invoked at
startup and then again at shutdown.

This is designed to be used as a decorator. An example usage,

```python
@app.while_serving
async def func():
    ...  # Startup
    yield
    ...  # Shutdown
```

Arguments:

: func: The function itself.

methods.name: quart.app.Quart.after_serving
documentation:
Add a after serving function.

This will allow the function provided to be called once after
anything is served (after last byte is sent).

This is designed to be used as a decorator, if used to
decorate a synchronous function, the function will be wrapped
in {func}`~quart.utils.run_sync` and run in a thread executor
(with the wrapped function returned). An example usage,

```python
@app.after_serving
async def func():
    ...
```

Arguments:

: func: The function itself.

methods.name: quart.app.Quart.create_url_adapter
documentation:
Create and return a URL adapter.

This will create the adapter based on the request if present
otherwise the app configuration.

methods.name: quart.app.Quart.websocket
documentation:
Add a websocket to the application.

This is designed to be used as a decorator, if used to
decorate a synchronous function, the function will be wrapped
in {func}`~quart.utils.run_sync` and run in a thread executor
(with the wrapped function returned). An example usage,

```python
@app.websocket('/')
async def websocket_route():
    ...
```

Arguments:

: rule: The path to route on, should start with a `/`.
  endpoint: Optional endpoint name, if not present the

  > function name is used.

  defaults: A dictionary of variables to provide automatically, use

  : to provide a simpler default path for a route, e.g. to allow
    for `/book` rather than `/book/0`,

    ```python
    @app.websocket('/book', defaults={'page': 0})
    @app.websocket('/book/<int:page>')
    def book(page):
        ...
    ```

  host: The full host name for this route (should include subdomain

  : if needed) - cannot be used with subdomain.

  subdomain: A subdomain for this specific route.
  strict_slashes: Strictly match the trailing slash present in the

  > path. Will redirect a leaf (no slash) to a branch (with slash).

methods.name: quart.app.Quart.add_websocket
documentation:
Add a websocket url rule to the application.

This is designed to be used on the application directly. An
example usage,

```python
def websocket_route():
    ...

app.add_websocket('/', websocket_route)
```

Arguments:

: rule: The path to route on, should start with a `/`.
  endpoint: Optional endpoint name, if not present the

  > function name is used.

  view_func: Callable that returns a response.
  defaults: A dictionary of variables to provide automatically, use

  > to provide a simpler default path for a route, e.g. to allow
  > for `/book` rather than `/book/0`,
  >
  > ```python
  > @app.websocket('/book', defaults={'page': 0})
  > @app.websocket('/book/<int:page>')
  > def book(page):
  >     ...
  > ```

  host: The full host name for this route (should include subdomain

  : if needed) - cannot be used with subdomain.

  subdomain: A subdomain for this specific route.
  strict_slashes: Strictly match the trailing slash present in the

  > path. Will redirect a leaf (no slash) to a branch (with slash).

methods.name: quart.app.Quart.url_for
documentation:
Return the url for a specific endpoint.

This is most useful in templates and redirects to create a URL
that can be used in the browser.

Arguments:

: endpoint: The endpoint to build a url for, if prefixed with

  : `.` it targets endpoint's in the current blueprint.

  \_anchor: Additional anchor text to append (i.e. #text).
  \_external: Return an absolute url for external (to app) usage.
  \_method: The method to consider alongside the endpoint.
  \_scheme: A specific scheme to use.
  values: The values to build into the URL, as specified in

  > the endpoint rule.

methods.name: quart.app.Quart.make_shell_context
documentation:
Create a context for interactive shell usage.

The {attr}`shell_context_processors` can be used to add
additional context.

methods.name: quart.app.Quart.run
documentation:
Run this application.

This is best used for development only, see Hypercorn for
production servers.

Arguments:
    host: Hostname to listen on. By default this is loopback
        only, use 0.0.0.0 to have the server listen externally.
    port: Port number to listen on.
    debug: If set enable (or disable) debug mode and debug output.
    use_reloader: Automatically reload on code changes.
    loop: Asyncio loop to create the server in, if None, take default one.
        If specified it is the caller's responsibility to close and cleanup the
        loop.
    ca_certs: Path to the SSL CA certificate file.
    certfile: Path to the SSL certificate file.
    keyfile: Path to the SSL key file.

methods.name: quart.app.Quart.run_task
documentation:
Return a task that when awaited runs this application.

This is best used for development only, see Hypercorn for
production servers.

Arguments:
    host: Hostname to listen on. By default this is loopback
        only, use 0.0.0.0 to have the server listen externally.
    port: Port number to listen on.
    debug: If set enable (or disable) debug mode and debug output.
    ca_certs: Path to the SSL CA certificate file.
    certfile: Path to the SSL certificate file.
    keyfile: Path to the SSL key file.

methods.name: quart.app.Quart.test_client
documentation: Creates and returns a test client.

methods.name: quart.app.Quart.test_cli_runner
documentation: Creates and returns a CLI test runner.

methods.name: quart.app.Quart.before_websocket
documentation:
Add a before websocket function.

This is designed to be used as a decorator, if used to
decorate a synchronous function, the function will be wrapped
in {func}`~quart.utils.run_sync` and run in a thread executor
(with the wrapped function returned). An example usage,

```python
@app.before_websocket
async def func():
    ...
```

Arguments:

: func: The before websocket function itself.

methods.name: quart.app.Quart.after_websocket
documentation:
Add an after websocket function.

This is designed to be used as a decorator, if used to
decorate a synchronous function, the function will be wrapped
in {func}`~quart.utils.run_sync` and run in a thread executor
(with the wrapped function returned). An example usage,

```python
@app.after_websocket
async def func(response):
    return response
```

Arguments:

: func: The after websocket function itself.

methods.name: quart.app.Quart.teardown_websocket
documentation:
Add a teardown websocket function.
This is designed to be used as a decorator, if used to
decorate a synchronous function, the function will be wrapped
in {func}`~quart.utils.run_sync` and run in a thread executor
(with the wrapped function returned). An example usage,
.. code-block:: python

> @app.teardown_websocket
> async def func():
>
> > ...

Arguments:

: func: The teardown websocket function itself.

methods.name: quart.app.Quart.handle_http_exception
documentation:
Handle a HTTPException subclass error.

This will attempt to find a handler for the error and if fails
will fall back to the error response.

methods.name: quart.app.Quart.handle_user_exception
documentation:
Handle an exception that has been raised.

This should forward {class}`~quart.exception.HTTPException` to
{meth}`handle_http_exception`, then attempt to handle the
error. If it cannot it should reraise the error.

methods.name: quart.app.Quart.handle_exception
documentation:
Handle an uncaught exception.

By default this switches the error response to a 500 internal
server error.

methods.name: quart.app.Quart.handle_websocket_exception
documentation:
Handle an uncaught exception.

By default this logs the exception and then re-raises it.

methods.name: quart.app.Quart.log_exception

type.name: tuple[type, BaseException, TracebackType] | tuple[None, None, None]

documentation:
Log a exception to the {attr}`logger`.

By default this is only invoked for unhandled exceptions.

methods.name: quart.app.Quart.ensure_async
documentation:
Ensure that the returned func is async and calls the func.

:::{versionadded} 0.11
:::

Override if you wish to change how synchronous functions are
run. Before Quart 0.11 this did not run the synchronous code
in an executor.

methods.name: quart.app.Quart.sync_to_async
documentation:
Return a async function that will run the synchronous function *func*.

This can be used as so,:

```
result = await app.sync_to_async(func)(*args, **kwargs)
```

Override this method to change how the app converts sync code
to be asynchronously callable.

methods.name: quart.app.Quart.do_teardown_request
documentation:
Teardown the request, calling the teardown functions.

Arguments:
    exc: Any exception not handled that has caused the request
        to teardown.
    request_context: The request context, optional as Flask
        omits this argument.

methods.name: quart.app.Quart.do_teardown_websocket
documentation:
Teardown the websocket, calling the teardown functions.

Arguments:
    exc: Any exception not handled that has caused the websocket
        to teardown.
    websocket_context: The websocket context, optional as Flask
        omits this argument.

methods.name: quart.app.Quart.do_teardown_appcontext
documentation: Teardown the app (context), calling the teardown functions.

methods.name: quart.app.Quart.app_context
documentation:
Create and return an app context.

This is best used within a context, i.e.

```python
async with app.app_context():
    ...
```

methods.name: quart.app.Quart.request_context
documentation:
Create and return a request context.

Use the {meth}`test_request_context` whilst testing. This is
best used within a context, i.e.

```python
async with app.request_context(request):
    ...
```

Arguments:

: request: A request to build a context around.

methods.name: quart.app.Quart.websocket_context
documentation:
Create and return a websocket context.

Use the {meth}`test_websocket_context` whilst testing. This is
best used within a context, i.e.

```python
async with app.websocket_context(websocket):
    ...
```

Arguments:

: websocket: A websocket to build a context around.

methods.name: quart.app.Quart.test_request_context
documentation:
Create a request context for testing purposes.

This is best used for testing code within request contexts. It
is a simplified wrapper of {meth}`request_context`. It is best
used in a with block, i.e.

```python
async with app.test_request_context("/", method="GET"):
    ...
```

Arguments:

: path: Request path.
  method: HTTP verb
  headers: Headers to include in the request.
  query_string: To send as a dictionary, alternatively the

  > query_string can be determined from the path.

  scheme: Scheme for the request, default http.

methods.name: quart.app.Quart.make_default_options_response
documentation: This is the default route function for OPTIONS requests.

methods.name: quart.app.Quart.make_response
documentation:
Make a Response from the result of the route handler.

The result itself can either be:
  - A Response object (or subclass).
  - A tuple of a ResponseValue and a header dictionary.
  - A tuple of a ResponseValue, status code and a header dictionary.

A ResponseValue is either a Response object (or subclass) or a str.

methods.name: quart.app.Quart.full_dispatch_request
documentation:
Adds pre and post processing to the request dispatching.

Arguments:
    request_context: The request context, optional as Flask
        omits this argument.

methods.name: quart.app.Quart.full_dispatch_websocket
documentation:
Adds pre and post processing to the websocket dispatching.

Arguments:
    websocket_context: The websocket context, optional to match
        the Flask convention.

methods.name: quart.app.Quart.preprocess_request
documentation:
Preprocess the request i.e. call before_request functions.

Arguments:
    request_context: The request context, optional as Flask
        omits this argument.

methods.name: quart.app.Quart.preprocess_websocket
documentation:
Preprocess the websocket i.e. call before_websocket functions.

Arguments:
    websocket_context: The websocket context, optional as Flask
        omits this argument.

methods.name: quart.app.Quart.dispatch_request
documentation:
Dispatch the request to the view function.

Arguments:
    request_context: The request context, optional as Flask
        omits this argument.

methods.name: quart.app.Quart.dispatch_websocket
documentation:
Dispatch the websocket to the view function.

Arguments:
    websocket_context: The websocket context, optional to match
        the Flask convention.

methods.name: quart.app.Quart.finalize_request
documentation:
Turns the view response return value into a response.

Arguments:
    result: The result of the request to finalize into a response.
    request_context: The request context, optional as Flask
        omits this argument.

methods.name: quart.app.Quart.finalize_websocket
documentation:
Turns the view response return value into a response.

Arguments:
    result: The result of the websocket to finalize into a response.
    websocket_context: The websocket context, optional as Flask
        omits this argument.

methods.name: quart.app.Quart.process_response
documentation:
Postprocess the request acting on the response.

Arguments:
    response: The response after the request is finalized.
    request_context: The request context, optional as Flask
        omits this argument.

methods.name: quart.app.Quart.postprocess_websocket
documentation:
Postprocess the websocket acting on the response.

Arguments:
    response: The response after the websocket is finalized.
    websocket_context: The websocket context, optional as Flask
        omits this argument.

methods.name: quart.app.Quart.__call__
documentation:
Called by ASGI servers.

The related {meth}`~quart.app.Quart.asgi_app` is called,
allowing for middleware usage whilst keeping the top level app
a {class}`~quart.app.Quart` instance.

methods.name: quart.app.Quart.asgi_app
documentation:
This handles ASGI calls, it can be wrapped in middleware.

When using middleware with Quart it is preferable to wrap this
method rather than the app itself. This is to ensure that the
app is an instance of this class - which allows the quart cli
to work correctly. To use this feature simply do,

```python
app.asgi_app = middleware(app.asgi_app)
```

class_variables.name: quart.app.Quart.test_client_class
documentation: type: ignore[assignment]

class_variables.name: quart.app.Quart.jinja_environment
documentation: type: ignore[assignment]

class_variables.name: quart.app.Quart.test_client_class
documentation: type: ignore[assignment]

class_variables.name: quart.app.Quart.test_cli_runner_class
documentation: type: ignore

class_variables.name: quart.app.Quart.url_rule_class
documentation: type: ignore[assignment]

The web framework class, handles requests and returns responses.

The primary method from a serving viewpoint is
{meth}`~quart.app.Quart.handle_request`, from an application
viewpoint all the other methods are vital.

This can be extended in many ways, with most methods designed with
this in mind. Additionally any of the classes listed as attributes
can be replaced.

Attributes:

: aborter_class: The class to use to raise HTTP error via the abort

  : helper function.

  app_ctx_globals_class: The class to use for the `g` object
  asgi_http_class: The class to use to handle the ASGI HTTP

  > protocol.

  asgi_lifespan_class: The class to use to handle the ASGI

  : lifespan protocol.

  asgi_websocket_class: The class to use to handle the ASGI

  : websocket protocol.

  config_class: The class to use for the configuration.
  env: The name of the environment the app is running on.
  event_class: The class to use to signal an event in an async

  > manner.

  debug: Wrapper around configuration DEBUG value, in many places

  : this will result in more output if True. If unset, debug
    mode will be activated if environ is set to 'development'.

  jinja_environment: The class to use for the jinja environment.
  jinja_options: The default options to set when creating the jinja

  > environment.

  permanent_session_lifetime: Wrapper around configuration

  : PERMANENT_SESSION_LIFETIME value. Specifies how long the session
    data should survive.

  request_class: The class to use for requests.
  response_class: The class to user for responses.
  secret_key: Warpper around configuration SECRET_KEY value. The app

  > secret for signing sessions.

  session_interface: The class to use as the session interface.
  shutdown_event: This event is set when the app starts to

  > shutdown allowing waiting tasks to know when to stop.

  url_map_class: The class to map rules to endpoints.
  url_rule_class: The class to use for URL rules.
  websocket_class: The class to use for websockets.

classes.name: quart.views.View

methods.name: quart.views.View.dispatch_request
documentation:
Override and return a Response.

This will be called with the request view_args, i.e. any url
parameters.

Use to define routes within a class structure.

A View subclass must implement the {meth}`dispatch_request` in
order to respond to requests. For automatic method finding based on
the request HTTP Verb see {class}`MethodView`.

An example usage is,

```python
class SimpleView:
    methods = ['GET']

    async def dispatch_request(id):
        return f"ID is {id}"

app.add_url_rule('/<id>', view_func=SimpleView.as_view('simple'))
```

Note that class

Attributes:

: decorators: A list of decorators to apply to a view

  : method. The decorators are applied in the order of
    the list.

  methods: List of methods this view allows.
  provide_automatic_options: Override automatic OPTIONS

  > if set, to either True or False.

  init_every_request: Create a new instance of this class

  : for every request.

classes.name: quart.views.MethodView
documentation:
A HTTP Method (verb) specific view class.

This has an implementation of {meth}`dispatch_request` such that
it calls a method based on the verb i.e. GET requests are handled
by a `get` method. For example,

```python
class SimpleView(MethodView):
    async def get(id):
        return f"Get {id}"

    async def post(id):
        return f"Post {id}"

  app.add_url_rule('/<id>', view_func=SimpleView.as_view('simple'))
```

methods.name: quart.testing.client.QuartClient.delete
documentation:
Make a DELETE request.

See {meth}`~quart.testing.QuartClient.open` for argument
details.

methods.name: quart.testing.client.QuartClient.get
documentation:
Make a GET request.

See {meth}`~quart.testing.QuartClient.open` for argument
details.

methods.name: quart.testing.client.QuartClient.head
documentation:
Make a HEAD request.

See {meth}`~quart.testing.QuartClient.open` for argument
details.

methods.name: quart.testing.client.QuartClient.options
documentation:
Make a OPTIONS request.

See {meth}`~quart.testing.QuartClient.open` for argument
details.

methods.name: quart.testing.client.QuartClient.patch
documentation:
Make a PATCH request.

See {meth}`~quart.testing.QuartClient.open` for argument
details.

methods.name: quart.testing.client.QuartClient.post
documentation:
Make a POST request.

See {meth}`~quart.testing.QuartClient.open` for argument
details.

methods.name: quart.testing.client.QuartClient.put
documentation:
Make a PUT request.

See {meth}`~quart.testing.QuartClient.open` for argument
details.

methods.name: quart.testing.client.QuartClient.trace
documentation:
Make a TRACE request.

See {meth}`~quart.testing.QuartClient.open` for argument
details.

methods.name: quart.testing.client.QuartClient.set_cookie
documentation:
Set a cookie in the cookie jar.

The arguments are the standard cookie morsels and this is a
wrapper around the stdlib SimpleCookie code.

methods.name: quart.testing.client.QuartClient.delete_cookie
documentation: Delete a cookie (set to expire immediately).

variables.name: quart.globals.session
documentation: type: ignore[assignment]

functions.name: quart.logging._setup_logging_queue
documentation: Create a new LocalQueueHandler and start an associated QueueListener.

functions.name: quart.logging.has_level_handler
documentation: Check if the logger already has a handler

functions.name: quart.logging.create_logger
documentation:
Create a logger for the app based on the app settings.

This creates a logger named quart.app that has a log level based
on the app configuration.

classes.name: quart.logging.LocalQueueHandler
documentation:
Custom QueueHandler that skips record preparation.

There is no need to prepare records that go into a local, in-process queue,
we can skip that process and minimise the cost of logging further.

methods.name: quart.wrappers.websocket.Websocket.__init__
documentation:
Create a request object.

Arguments:
    path: The full unquoted path of the request.
    query_string: The raw bytes for the query string part.
    scheme: The scheme used for the request.
    headers: The request headers.
    root_path: The root path that should be prepended to all
        routes.
    http_version: The HTTP version of the request.
    subprotocols: The subprotocols requested.
    receive: Returns an awaitable of the current data
    accept: Idempotent callable to accept the websocket connection.
    scope: Underlying ASGI scope dictionary.

methods.name: quart.wrappers.websocket.Websocket.accept
documentation:
Manually chose to accept the websocket connection.

Arguments:
    headers: Additional headers to send with the acceptance
        response.
    subprotocol: The chosen subprotocol, optional.

classes.name: quart.blueprints.Blueprint

methods.name: quart.blueprints.Blueprint.get_send_file_max_age
documentation:
Used by {func}`send_file` to determine the `max_age` cache
value for a given file path if it wasn't passed.

By default, this returns {data}`SEND_FILE_MAX_AGE_DEFAULT` from
the configuration of {data}`~flask.current_app`. This defaults
to `None`, which tells the browser to use conditional requests
instead of a timed cache, which is usually preferable.

Note this is a duplicate of the same method in the Quart
class.

methods.name: quart.blueprints.Blueprint.open_resource
documentation:
Open a file for reading.

Use as

```python
async with await app.open_resource(path) as file_:
    await file_.read()
```

methods.name: quart.blueprints.Blueprint.websocket
documentation:
Add a websocket to the application.

This is designed to be used as a decorator, if used to
decorate a synchronous function, the function will be wrapped
in {func}`~quart.utils.run_sync` and run in a thread executor
(with the wrapped function returned). An example usage,

```python
@app.websocket('/')
async def websocket_route():
    ...
```

Arguments:

: rule: The path to route on, should start with a `/`.
  endpoint: Optional endpoint name, if not present the

  > function name is used.

  defaults: A dictionary of variables to provide automatically, use

  : to provide a simpler default path for a route, e.g. to allow
    for `/book` rather than `/book/0`,

    ```python
    @app.websocket('/book', defaults={'page': 0})
    @app.websocket('/book/<int:page>')
    def book(page):
        ...
    ```

  host: The full host name for this route (should include subdomain

  : if needed) - cannot be used with subdomain.

  subdomain: A subdomain for this specific route.
  strict_slashes: Strictly match the trailing slash present in the

  > path. Will redirect a leaf (no slash) to a branch (with slash).

methods.name: quart.blueprints.Blueprint.add_websocket
documentation:
Add a websocket url rule to the application.

This is designed to be used on the application directly. An
example usage,

```python
def websocket_route():
    ...

app.add_websocket('/', websocket_route)
```

Arguments:

: rule: The path to route on, should start with a `/`.
  endpoint: Optional endpoint name, if not present the

  > function name is used.

  view_func: Callable that returns a response.
  defaults: A dictionary of variables to provide automatically, use

  > to provide a simpler default path for a route, e.g. to allow
  > for `/book` rather than `/book/0`,
  >
  > ```python
  > @app.websocket('/book', defaults={'page': 0})
  > @app.websocket('/book/<int:page>')
  > def book(page):
  >     ...
  > ```

  host: The full host name for this route (should include subdomain

  : if needed) - cannot be used with subdomain.

  subdomain: A subdomain for this specific route.
  strict_slashes: Strictly match the trailing slash present in the

  > path. Will redirect a leaf (no slash) to a branch (with slash).

methods.name: quart.blueprints.Blueprint.before_websocket
documentation:
Add a before websocket function.

This is designed to be used as a decorator, if used to
decorate a synchronous function, the function will be wrapped
in {func}`~quart.utils.run_sync` and run in a thread executor
(with the wrapped function returned). An example usage,

```python
@app.before_websocket
async def func():
    ...
```

Arguments:

: func: The before websocket function itself.

methods.name: quart.blueprints.Blueprint.after_websocket
documentation:
Add an after websocket function.

This is designed to be used as a decorator, if used to
decorate a synchronous function, the function will be wrapped
in {func}`~quart.utils.run_sync` and run in a thread executor
(with the wrapped function returned). An example usage,

```python
@app.after_websocket
async def func(response):
    return response
```

Arguments:

: func: The after websocket function itself.

methods.name: quart.blueprints.Blueprint.teardown_websocket
documentation:
Add a teardown websocket function.
This is designed to be used as a decorator, if used to
decorate a synchronous function, the function will be wrapped
in {func}`~quart.utils.run_sync` and run in a thread executor
(with the wrapped function returned). An example usage,
.. code-block:: python

> @app.teardown_websocket
> async def func():
>
> > ...

Arguments:

: func: The teardown websocket function itself.
  name: Optional blueprint key name.

methods.name: quart.blueprints.Blueprint.before_app_websocket
documentation:
Add a before websocket to the App.

This is designed to be used as a decorator, and has the same arguments
as {meth}`~quart.Quart.before_websocket`. It applies to all requests to the
app this blueprint is registered on. An example usage,

```python
blueprint = Blueprint(__name__)
@blueprint.before_app_websocket
def before():
    ...
```

methods.name: quart.blueprints.Blueprint.before_app_serving
documentation:
Add a before serving to the App.

This is designed to be used as a decorator, and has the same arguments
as {meth}`~quart.Quart.before_serving`. An example usage,

```python
blueprint = Blueprint(__name__)
@blueprint.before_app_serving
def before():
    ...
```

methods.name: quart.blueprints.Blueprint.after_app_websocket
documentation:
Add an after websocket function to the App.

This is designed to be used as a decorator, and has the same arguments
as {meth}`~quart.Quart.after_websocket`. It applies to all requests to the
ppe this blueprint is registered on. An example usage,

```python
blueprint = Blueprint(__name__)
@blueprint.after_app_websocket
def after():
    ...
```

methods.name: quart.blueprints.Blueprint.after_app_serving
documentation:
Add an after serving function to the App.

This is designed to be used as a decorator, and has the same arguments
as {meth}`~quart.Quart.after_serving`. An example usage,

```python
blueprint = Blueprint(__name__)
@blueprint.after_app_serving
def after():
    ...
```

methods.name: quart.blueprints.Blueprint.while_app_serving
documentation:
Add a while serving function to the App.

This is designed to be used as a decorator, and has the same arguments
as {meth}`~quart.Quart.while_serving`. An example usage,

```python
@blueprint.while_serving
async def func():
    ...  # Startup
    yield
    ...  # Shutdown
```

methods.name: quart.blueprints.Blueprint.teardown_app_websocket
documentation:
Add a teardown websocket function to the app.

This is designed to be used as a decorator, and has the same
arguments as {meth}`~quart.Quart.teardown_websocket`. It applies
to all requests to the app this blueprint is registered on. An
example usage,

```python
blueprint = Blueprint(__name__)
@blueprint.teardown_app_websocket
def teardown():
    ...
```

A blueprint is a collection of application properties.

The application properties include routes, error handlers, and
before and after request functions. It is useful to produce
modular code as it allows the properties to be defined in a
blueprint thereby deferring the addition of these properties to the
app.

classes.name: quart.wrappers.base.BaseRequestWebsocket

methods.name: quart.wrappers.base.BaseRequestWebsocket.__init__
documentation:
Create a request or websocket base object.

Arguments:
    method: The HTTP verb.
    scheme: The scheme used for the request.
    path: The full unquoted path of the request.
    query_string: The raw bytes for the query string part.
    headers: The request headers.
    root_path: The root path that should be prepended to all
        routes.
    http_version: The HTTP version of the request.
    scope: Underlying ASGI scope dictionary.

Attributes:
    args: The query string arguments.
    scheme: The URL scheme, http or https.

methods.name: quart.wrappers.base.BaseRequestWebsocket.max_content_length
documentation: Read-only view of the `MAX_CONTENT_LENGTH` config key.

methods.name: quart.wrappers.base.BaseRequestWebsocket.endpoint
documentation:
Returns the corresponding endpoint matched for this request.

This can be None if the request has not been matched with a
rule.

methods.name: quart.wrappers.base.BaseRequestWebsocket.blueprint
documentation:
Returns the blueprint the matched endpoint belongs to.

This can be None if the request has not been matched or the
endpoint is not in a blueprint.

methods.name: quart.wrappers.base.BaseRequestWebsocket.blueprints
documentation:
Return the names of the current blueprints.
The returned list is ordered from the current blueprint,
upwards through parent blueprints.

class_variables.name: quart.wrappers.base.BaseRequestWebsocket.json_module
documentation: type: ignore

This class is the basis for Requests and websockets..

Attributes:
    json_module: A custom json decoding/encoding module, it should
        have `dump`, `dumps`, `load`, and `loads` methods
    routing_exception: If an exception is raised during the route
        matching it will be stored here.
    url_rule: The rule that this request has been matched too.
    view_args: The keyword arguments for the view from the route
        matching.

classes.name: quart.wrappers.request.Body

methods.name: quart.wrappers.request.Body.set_result
documentation: Convenience method, mainly for testing.

A request body container.

The request body can either be iterated over and consumed in parts
(without building up memory usage) or awaited.

```python
async for data in body:
    ...
# or simply
complete = await body
```

Note: It is not possible to iterate over the data and then await
it.

classes.name: quart.wrappers.request.Request

methods.name: quart.wrappers.request.Request.__init__
documentation:
Create a request object.

Arguments:

: method: The HTTP verb.
  scheme: The scheme used for the request.
  path: The full unquoted path of the request.
  query_string: The raw bytes for the query string part.
  headers: The request headers.
  root_path: The root path that should be prepended to all

  > routes.

  http_version: The HTTP version of the request.
  body: An awaitable future for the body data i.e.

  > `data = await body`

  max_content_length: The maximum length in bytes of the

  : body (None implies no limit in Quart).

  body_timeout: The maximum time (seconds) to wait for the

  : body before timing out.

  send_push_promise: An awaitable to send a push promise based

  : off of this request (HTTP/2 feature).

  scope: Underlying ASGI scope dictionary.

methods.name: quart.wrappers.request.Request.get_data
documentation:
Get the request body data.

Arguments:
    cache: If False the body data will be cleared, resulting in any
        subsequent calls returning an empty AnyStr and reducing
        memory usage.
    as_text: If True the data is returned as a decoded string,
        otherwise raw bytes are returned.
    parse_form_data: Parse the data as form data first, return any
        remaining data.

methods.name: quart.wrappers.request.Request.form
documentation:
The parsed form encoded data.

Note file data is present in the {attr}`files`.

methods.name: quart.wrappers.request.Request.files
documentation:
The parsed files.

This will return an empty multidict unless the request
mimetype was `enctype="multipart/form-data"` and the method
POST, PUT, or PATCH.

methods.name: quart.wrappers.request.Request.get_json
documentation:
Parses the body data as JSON and returns it.

Arguments:

: force: Force JSON parsing even if the mimetype is not JSON.
  silent: Do not trigger error handling if parsing fails, without

  > this the {meth}`on_json_loading_failed` will be called on
  > error.

  cache: Cache the parsed JSON on this request object.

methods.name: quart.wrappers.request.Request.on_json_loading_failed
documentation:
Handle a JSON parsing error.

Arguments:
    error: The exception raised during parsing.
Returns:
    Any value returned (if overridden) will be used as the
    default for any get_json calls.

This class represents a request.

It can be subclassed and the subclassed used in preference by
replacing the {attr}`~quart.Quart.request_class` with your
subclass.

Attributes:

: body_class: The class to store the body data within.
  form_data_parser_class: Can be overridden to implement a

  > different form data parsing.

functions.name: quart.ctx.after_this_request
quart.ctx.after_this_websocket
quart.ctx.copy_current_app_context
documentation:
Share the current app context with the function decorated.

The app context is local per task and hence will not be available
in any other task. This decorator can be used to make the context
available,

```python
@copy_current_app_context
async def within_context() -> None:
    name = current_app.name
    ...
```

functions.name: quart.ctx.copy_current_request_context
documentation:
Share the current request context with the function decorated.

The request context is local per task and hence will not be
available in any other task. This decorator can be used to make
the context available,

```python
@copy_current_request_context
async def within_context() -> None:
    method = request.method
    ...
```

functions.name: quart.ctx.copy_current_websocket_context
documentation:
Share the current websocket context with the function decorated.

The websocket context is local per task and hence will not be
available in any other task. This decorator can be used to make
the context available,

```python
@copy_current_websocket_context
async def within_context() -> None:
    method = websocket.method
    ...
```

functions.name: quart.ctx.has_app_context
documentation:
Check if execution is within an app context.

This allows a controlled way to act if there is an app context
available, or silently not act if not. For example,

```python
if has_app_context():
    log.info("Executing in %s context", current_app.name)
```

See also {func}`has_request_context`

functions.name: quart.ctx.has_request_context
documentation:
Check if execution is within a request context.

This allows a controlled way to act if there is a request context
available, or silently not act if not. For example,

```python
if has_request_context():
    log.info("Request endpoint %s", request.endpoint)
```

See also {func}`has_app_context`.

functions.name: quart.ctx.has_websocket_context
documentation:
Check if execution is within a websocket context.

This allows a controlled way to act if there is a websocket
context available, or silently not act if not. For example,

```python
if has_websocket_context():
    log.info("Websocket endpoint %s", websocket.endpoint)
```

See also {func}`has_app_context`.

classes.name: quart.ctx._BaseRequestWebsocketContext

methods.name: quart.ctx._BaseRequestWebsocketContext.match_request
documentation:
Match the request against the adapter.

Override this method to configure request matching, it should
set the request url_rule and view_args and optionally a
routing_exception.

A base context relating to either request or websockets, bound to the current task.

Attributes:
    app: The app itself.
    request_websocket: The request or websocket itself.
    url_adapter: An adapter bound to this request.
    session: The session information relating to this request.

classes.name: quart.ctx.RequestContext
documentation:
The context relating to the specific request, bound to the current task.

Do not use directly, prefer the
{func}`~quart.Quart.request_context` or
{func}`~quart.Quart.test_request_context` instead.

Attributes:
: \_after_request_functions: List of functions to execute after the current

  : request, see {func}`after_this_request`.

classes.name: quart.ctx.WebsocketContext
documentation:
The context relating to the specific websocket, bound to the current task.

Do not use directly, prefer the
{func}`~quart.Quart.websocket_context` or
{func}`~quart.Quart.test_websocket_context` instead.

Attributes:
: \_after_websocket_functions: List of functions to execute after the current

  : websocket, see {func}`after_this_websocket`.

classes.name: quart.ctx.AppContext
documentation:
The context relating to the app bound to the current task.

Do not use directly, prefer the
{func}`~quart.Quart.app_context` instead.

Attributes:

: app: The app itself.
  url_adapter: An adapter bound to the server, but not a

  > specific task, useful for route building.

  g: An instance of the ctx globals class.

functions.name: quart.cli._called_with_wrong_args
documentation:
Check whether calling a function raised a `TypeError` because
the call failed or because something in the factory raised the
error.
\:param f: The function that was called.
\:return: `True` if the call failed.

functions.name: quart.cli.prepare_import
documentation:
Given a filename this will try to calculate the python path, add it
to the search path and return the actual module name that is expected.

functions.name: quart.cli.load_dotenv
documentation:
Load "dotenv" files in order of precedence to set environment variables.
If an env var is already set it is not overwritten, so earlier files in the
list are preferred over later files.
This is a no-op if [python-dotenv] is not installed.
.. \_python-dotenv: <https://github.com/theskumar/python-dotenv#readme>
\:param path: Load the file at this location instead of searching.
\:return: `True` if a file was loaded.

functions.name: quart.cli.run_command
documentation: Run a local development server.

functions.name: quart.cli.shell_command
documentation:
Run an interactive Python shell in the context of a given
Quart application.  The application will populate the default
namespace of this shell according to its configuration.
This is useful for executing small snippets of management code
without having to manually configure the application.

functions.name: quart.cli.routes_command
documentation: Show all registered routes with endpoints and methods.

classes.name: quart.cli.AppGroup

methods.name: quart.cli.AppGroup.command
documentation:
This works exactly like the method of the same name on a regular
{class}`click.Group` but it wraps callbacks in {func}`with_appcontext`
if it's enabled by passing `with_appcontext=True`.

This works similar to a regular click {class}`~click.Group` but it
changes the behavior of the {meth}`command` decorator so that it
automatically wraps the functions in {func}`with_appcontext`.

Not to be confused with {class}`QuartGroup`.

classes.name: quart.datastructures.FileStorage

methods.name: quart.datastructures.FileStorage.save
documentation:
Save the file to the destination.

Arguments:
    destination: A filename (str) or file object to write to.
    buffer_size: Buffer size to keep in memory.
A thin wrapper over incoming files.

methods.name: quart.config.Config.from_prefixed_env
documentation:
Load any environment variables that start with the prefix.

The prefix (default `QUART_`) is dropped from the env key
for the config key. Values are passed through a loading
function to attempt to convert them to more specific types
than strings.

Keys are loaded in {func}`sorted` order.

The default loading function attempts to parse values as any
valid JSON type, including dicts and lists.  Specific items in
nested dicts can be set by separating the keys with double
underscores (`__`). If an intermediate key doesn't exist, it
will be initialized to an empty dict.

Arguments:
: prefix: Load env vars that start with this prefix,

  : separated with an underscore (`_`).

  loads: Pass each string value to this function and use the

  : returned value as the config value. If any error is
    raised it is ignored and the value remains a
    string. The default is {func}`json.loads`.

classes.name: quart.wrappers.response.ResponseBody
documentation:
Base class wrapper for response body data.

This ensures that the following is possible (as Quart assumes so
when returning the body to the ASGI server

    async with wrapper as response:
        async for data in response:
            send(data)

classes.name: quart.wrappers.response.FileBody
documentation:
Provides an async file accessor with range setting.

The {attr}`Response.response` attribute must be async-iterable and
yield bytes, which this wrapper does for a file. In addition it
allows a range to be set on the file, thereby supporting
conditional requests.

Attributes:

: buffer_size: Size in bytes to load per iteration.

classes.name: quart.wrappers.response.IOBody
documentation:
Provides an async file accessor with range setting.

The {attr}`Response.response` attribute must be async-iterable and
yield bytes, which this wrapper does for a file. In addition it
allows a range to be set on the file, thereby supporting
conditional requests.

Attributes:

: buffer_size: Size in bytes to load per iteration.

classes.name: quart.wrappers.response.Response

methods.name: quart.wrappers.response.Response.__init__
documentation:
Create a response object.

The response itself can be a chunk of data or a
iterable/generator of data chunks.

The Content-Type can either be specified as a mimetype or
content_type header or omitted to use the
{attr}`default_mimetype`.

Arguments:

: response: The response data or iterable over the data.
  status: Status code of the response.
  headers: Headers to attach to the response.
  mimetype: Mimetype of the response.
  content_type: Content-Type header value.

Attributes:

: response: An iterable of the response bytes-data.

methods.name: quart.wrappers.response.Response.get_data
documentation: Return the body data.

methods.name: quart.wrappers.response.Response.set_data
documentation:
Set the response data.

This will encode using the {attr}`charset`.

methods.name: quart.wrappers.response.Response.get_json
documentation:
Parses the body data as JSON and returns it.

Arguments:

: force: Force JSON parsing even if the mimetype is not JSON.
  silent: Do not trigger error handling if parsing fails, without

  > this the {meth}`on_json_loading_failed` will be called on
  > error.

methods.name: quart.wrappers.response.Response.freeze
documentation: Freeze this object ready for pickling.

This class represents a response.

It can be subclassed and the subclassed used in preference by
replacing the {attr}`~quart.Quart.response_class` with your
subclass.

Attributes:

: automatically_set_content_length: If False the content length

  : header must be provided.

  default_status: The status code to use if not provided.
  default_mimetype: The mimetype to use if not provided.
  implicit_sequence_conversion: Implicitly convert the response

  > to a iterable in the get_data method, to allow multiple
  > iterations.
